commentBody:
My five favorite original clues from last week
(in order of appearance):

1. A little sun?  (5)(4)
2. Cue ... or queue  (4)
3. Semi professional?  (8)
4. Perpetual homebody?  (5)
5. What may begin at the end  (9)


DWARF STAR
LINE
TEAMSTER
SNAIL
AFTERLIFE
Fabulous opening puzzle to celebrate June!
This was hilarious. Well done, Kenneth!
Congrats Kenneth! Happy pride to you.
Terrific puzzle!  Happy Pride month, all.
Well, it definitely sets the tone for the month!  Had a fun time!
I loved solving this puzzle so much, happy pride!! 🏳️‍🌈 Especially in these times where queer people are facing so many difficulties, seeing this revealer made me smile
👏 Well done, Kenneth, and Happy Pride!
Congrats and excellent puzzle, Kenneth!

Congratulations, Kenneth!

May you find acceptance from your family, love from someone special, and joy from composing and solving puzzles.
Wonderful in every way. Congratulations, Kenneth, beautiful puzzle!
This puzzle put such a smile on my face. Happy Pride!
Love this puzzle and love that it was published! Thank you Kenneth for creating it and thank you NYT for choosing it as the opening puzzle for June.
Well, excellent! And, Mom, Dad, Kenneth’s also quite clever! I’ll bet you already knew that.

Such a great way to kick off Pride Month! We heard those exact words from our daughter a few years ago and it's been great to see how liberating it has been for her to be her true self. My heart goes out to those with non-affirming parents. You are worthy of love and acceptance!
Great puzzle, Kenneth and congratulations on that reveal!

I hadn't really noticed the grayed squares until just now, as I was reviewing the puzzle.

Oh, wow!  Oh, wow!  Oh, wow!  Oh, wow!  Oh, wow!

Along with Pride Month, it ought to be considered Courage Month.  Takes some guts in this environment.
Congratulations! This must feel like a weight has been lifted for you. Very brave, and what a lovely way to come out.
Congratulations Kenneth!
This made me smile. Congrats Kenneth!
Congrats Kenneth! 🏳️‍🌈

It seems fitting that AURA is smack in the center of the grid. 

And if this boomer understands correctly, doing something impressive earns your “aura points” in Gen Alpha lingo.  So congratulations, Kenneth, on a big time score.

(High score on the puzzle CLAPOMETER too).

I like how the highlighted words – MOM, DAD, I’M, QUEER – are not hidden or masked within the answers they’re in.  That is, the answer that MOM was in, wasn’t, say, MOMENTOUS.

No, MOM means “mom” in MOMJEANS, DAD means “dad” in DADBOD, I’M means “I’m” in I’M THERE, and QUEER means “queer” in QUEER EYE.

It makes the theme all the more powerful and moving.

When you got my heart involved, as well as my brain, you made the experience memorable and special.  Thank you so much for this, Kenneth!

At first I thought that this puzzle was a bit queer and I truly meant no disrespect! Now after finishing it I realize it really was! Congratulations Kenneth on an eye opening, historic/historical puzzle! And Thank you Sam for your great commentary!
Loved it! 🏳️‍🌈

I especially appreciate the image that the mom and dad in this story might be “uncool”, what with their mom jeans and dad bods, but still as cool as it gets when loving their child for who they are.
Happy Pride to all.
Sexuality and gender identity aside, I had a good chuckle when I got the reveal.
Well done, Kenneth. Wishing all the love and support you need, and then some.

Thank you, Kenneth Cortes!

For putting yourself out there, for sharing so generously of yourself, and for constructing more than a crossword puzzle.

. . . .
I rarely comment on Monday puzzles but this one definitely qualifies as my favourite Monday puzzle from this year. Happy pride month to everyone!
Amazing puzzle
Delightful and clever! Thanks Ken.
Definitely a cool reveal!

I can relate, because the Mrs. and I have a son and a daughter, and have been part of that conversation twice. (Only with our daughter, it was "Mom, Dad, I'm bi.")  Son went first, and was relieved that not only was it no problem for us, but that we kind of knew by that point anyway.  Several years later, daughter's job was made easier  because she knew she wouldn't have anything to worry about when she came out.

You don't stop loving your kids.  This particular piece of information doesn't change how you feel about them at all. 

All you care about is that they are happy.  And as of Aug. 22, when said daughter gets married, we'll have both a son-in-law and a daughter-in-law.  Just not with the partners we originally expected.

And life goes on.  Because life is made up of all kinds of people.  And people should be happy if it is at all possible, and live their best lives.
Daww! Sweetest crossword ever. Congrats Kenneth, and happy pride month to all!
'Queer' can mean just about anything these days.

Kenneth, I wish you all good things and that you always encounter acceptance. 
Should acceptance be sadly lacking, may you in the very least encounter respect. 
And so, may your lifetime and paths be filled with decent human beings who know to look at a person's character only and nothing else.

Congratulations on a clever and humorous yet profoundly meaningful Monday puzzle.
This is quite the coming out party!! Congratulations, Kenneth!!

Well done, Kenneth! 

I’ve never commented, and may not again, but I’m an avid solver.  Your puzzle and the inclusiveness it brings brought me here to comment. Thanks for kicking off pride month in a clever and insightful way.  I even got my wife (not a crosser) to pause and appreciate it. 

Well done, and thanks for representing us so well.  Keep it up - we appreciate you!

Two fun entries crossing: 47A [Smokey Robinson's record label] MOTOWN Here's Smokey Robinson's first hit with Motown (in 1960)--"Shop Around" (also Motown's first million-selling hit record) "Try to get yourself a bargain, son Don't be sold on the very first one (On the first one, oh) Now, pretty girls come a dime a dozen Try to find you one who's gonna give you true loving... My mama told me, you better shop around" https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=54A_DH5K--U Big hit--earworm! 43D [Piña ___] COLADA I think we saw Piña Colada the other day as the national drink of Puerto Rico. Yum. I'll have another one. Pineapple and coconut--what could be bad?

Wonderful,  Kenneth!

Happy Pride Month everyone!
I was going to say this was a tough Monday, but once I realized the theme I realize it’s a very impressive Monday!

So, today’s homily was about human dignity. The deacon related a story from his past. His MIL was in the hospital awaiting an important test and it was taking forever. He went to the desk to inquire about the holdup and was told, “we only have one test kit and have to save it in case it’s needed by someone important.” He told them, “ok, someone important is here and needs it, and she’s in that room right over there.” 
His message: no one is more important than the person you are currently looking at, whomever that might be.

So, Kenneth, here’s looking at you, kid.
My nominee for Best Monday Puzzle Ever, thank you Kenneth!

I posted a reply in the Sunday puzzle's comments, but late in the day, so in case it wasn't seen, here is a copy paste that goes out to Heidi, Lewis, Tim, Marshall, Seward Parker, Whoa Nellie, and Vaer! https://www.nytimes.com/shared/comment/4gg16d?rsrc=cshare&smid=url-share

commentBody:
Overall level-of-difficulty-per-clue average greater than yesterday's grid.

That shouldn't be so for a Sunday-Monday set.

Just me?

As someone who has descendants who have gone through periods of uncertainty regarding the persons and genders they are attracted to, my major concern is how happy and safe they are in their relationships. As long as those conditions are met, the rest is none of my business. My wife and I love our daughters and granddaughters, and my hope is that they will be with people who will love them and treat them with the respect and love they deserve.

Living where I do, I am no stranger to Pride Month and its activities, I sincerely wish that there was no need to spotlight it or to celebrate any other f(r)action of the inhabitants of the country or the world because the rest of us have learned acceptance and tolerance; and not to be dismissive or afraid of folks who don't act or look like we do.

Congratulations on the reveals, both in your puzzle and in your life, and thank you for a nice start to Pride Month.

Aww wow that was special. I spent a second trying to understand the theme at the end, assuming it was just a normal Monday today - I was very wrong! I don't often read the constructor notes to be honest, but after finishing this one I obviously had to. Sincerely powerful and heartwarming.
Nice puzzle, too - I especially liked the geography crossers in the bottom right. As a huge countries and capitals nerd I always think there should be more geography in the NYT...
Thanks Kenneth, all the best wishes to you and everyone you will inspire with this gem of a crossword!

A Monday puzzle that's mildly challenging without going too far for the day, and with an actually interesting theme that's been perfectly incorporated into the grid? Nice!

I was really impressed by how the theme made a coherent whole, and one meaningful to the constructor, too. Only last week I commented how early-week themes usually do nothing for me, as they tend to be largely random assortments of phrases only tangentially linked by some arbitrary revealer, which has nothing to do with what the themed entries actually mean, beyond some cringingly punny pseudo-connection. Well, not so today! Great stuff.
This felt like being a part of a stranger’s life moment in the best (and most grammar nerdy) way possible! Wishing you a grid full of happiness Kenneth!

At first, I was amused by “MOM JEANS” and “DAD BODS” living side by side. Then I got the theme and, just like dad’s waistline, my heart grew a size or two. 

Thanks, Kenneth, for a puzzle that not only challenged my brain but also warmed my heart. That’s a pretty rare achievement. Happy Pride Month to you and everyone you know.
Congrats, Kenneth!  Mr. & Mrs. Cortes, your son rocks!

A truly special Monday, congratulations Kenneth - I got the revealer halfway through the solve and worked out what was going on, but I thought “surely not, this is too good to be true”… WordPlay confirms for me that it is true.

A remarkable crossword and moment, thank you Kenneth and NYT for happy gifts like this in a world that is increasingly in need of them.
This is an extraordinary puzzle, a themer that brings with it a celebration of acceptance.  Well done, Kenneth Cortes.  From courage comes pride.
Wonderful theme and revealer. Clever, personal, and very fun. Congratulations, Kenneth! Loved it.

Remarkable puzzle. 

Much praise and admiration, Mr. Cortes.

Best to you and yours (which is Minnesotan for "y'all").
A great puzzle! Happy Pride, everone!

My particular slice of pride: i finished it in 15:30 without using any of the Down clues! I only looked at the crosses. Yay me, and more importantly a huge Yay Everyone who is celebrating Pride month for whatever reason!

Just one quibble:

I merrily wrote DUKE as the North Carolina university. To my dismay, perhaps even horror, i discover that it is in fact ELON. I mean no disrespect to the faculty, students and alumni of Elon, but… is there anything that man can’t sully?

Excellent theme, Happy Pride Month!

NW & APEXAM provided some fair resistance but it's a breezy solve
Love the theme! Happy Pride Month! 🌈
Congrats, Kenneth! I audibly gasped when I figured the clue out. I'm proud of you!
Happy Pride!  Great puzzle.  Loved it.
This was very cool. Good for the constructor!

Saw that message and I wondered if the constructor actually DID use a puzzle to come out to his parents. Kenneth, may you find the love of your life if you haven't already done so. And now you have come out to all of us who did your puzzle. HAPPY PRIDE MONTH!
Very cute ☺️ Happy pride month, everyone!
Man, really loved the shaded squares + revealer. Excellent and well done.
Love it! Happy Pride! 🌈
Excellent in every way. I echo the positive and congratulatory sentiments already expressed.

Damn, that was amazing.  My hat is off to you, Kenneth, for your pride, your bravery and your crossword skills.  This is a puzzle that is certain to get talked about for a long time (yes, it is historic).  You'll be a tough act to follow for whoever constructed the Tuesday this week.  Which, damn you Kenneth, is me... :-)

I enjoyed the puzzle. Crunchy for a Monday. It was unclear if this puzzle was the actual mechanism by which the constructor came out to their parents, which obviously doesn't matter but I'm curious. 

Happy Pride everybody. Take up space. Damn the man.
Oh this was clever! Didn't get the gag at all until the end. Sharp for a Monday, thank you!
Thanks for the fabulous puzzle.  Loved it!

Love it!  

Happy pride, all!

What an amazing Monday puzzle. Slowly reading across the top and appreciating the theme in all it's glory as it revealed itself was heartwarming. Congratulations on a puzzle well done. Take pride in your puzzle and your message.
Very sweet. Thank you!
Loved it! Heartwarming to read all of the love and pride in the comments. Both solving and reading brought tears to my eyes. Happy Pride Month! Love wins!
Smokey Robinson’s records, both with The Miracles and solo, were released on the TAMLA label.  MOTOWN was their record company, not their label.
Llanfairpwllgwyngyllgogerychwyrndrobwllllantysiliogogogoch
Lovely!

Lovely puzzle - thank you!!!

As a kiwi I must get a wee mention in that the longest place in the world is actually right here in Aotearoa:

The world-record holder officially recognized by Guinness World Records is Taumatawhakatangihangakoauauotamateaturipukakapikimaungahoronukupokaiwhenuakitanatahu (85 letters). Located on the North Island of New Zealand near Pōrangahau, it translates to English as "the place where Tamatea, the man with the big knees, who slid, climbed and swallowed mountains, played his flute to his loved one"

I hope this gift of a puzzle will be shared with moms and dads around the world from their children who may be struggling to say these words. 

And congratulations, Kenneth! This clever crossword left me with an "oh, WOW!" moment and a lasting smile. Happy Pride month!

Happy Pride Month! For those interested in the history of the theme, I recommend this article: https://theconversation.com/the-history-of-coming-out-from-secret-gay-code-to-popular-political-protest-129609
EXAM directly atop TEST was interesting.

Somebody chopping onions? 🥹
Happy pride everyone
Congratulations Kevin! What a beautiful (and clever!) puzzle. Such a perfect way to kick off Pride Month. 🌈
Congratulations Kenneth!! What a beautiful (and clever!) puzzle. Such a perfect way to kick off Pride Month. 🌈
was this harder than the usual monday puzzle or did i not get enough sleep?
Best Monday puzzle ever! Thank you for it.
So proud of you!

Beautiful.

Just beautiful.

To be blown away by a Monday puzzle is not something I was expecting. I usually do them just because they exist. But this one, wow, just wow. Not only an incredible theme with a revealer that brought tears to my eyes when I recognized the gravitas of it, but fill that's unusually sparkling for an early week puzzle. Absolutely brilliant.

Having done these puzzles for 60 years, I don't remember a lot of them. In fact, I can only think of one, the CLINTON/BOB DOLE WINS that made it into my "Most Memorable" list. This is now the second.

Kenneth, wishing you all the best, and Happy Pride Month. And thanks to the NYT for publishing it.
I needed to read Wordplay to get the theme (pre-morning coffee?). What a joyful, funi way to start Pride Month! Btw, hasn't there been a wedding proposal puzzle?

Fab vibe!

And congratulations to today's commentators for making this forum so uplifting and heartening!
Thank you for a wonderful puzzle, Kenneth!  Happy Pride, everyone!
Excellent puzzle! Congrats Kenneth! We love you.

DYSTOPIAs are often characterized by fear or distress, tyrannical governments, environmental disaster, or other characteristics associated with a cataclysmic decline in society. The clue could have been "What we're living in."
Best. Puzzle. Ever. Happy pride!!!
Congrats, Kenneth! Nerve-wracking indeed, but enjoy the relief and pride in your identity. Never forget that you have an international family at your side. Wishing you the best!
Congratulations Kenneth!!!! <3
Congrats! Happy Pride!

That'll wake you up on a Monday!

Congrats!  Must feel great!
You made my day, Kenneth!  All the best to you!  Happy PRIDE!!

commentBody: I'll join the chorus. Quite an amazing puzzle and a nice moment for the theme to finally dawn on me when I was almost done. And... appropriate puzzle find today - a Monday from May 8, 2017 by Zhouqin Burnikel. Some answers in that one: 18a - ELLEFANNING 24a - GEEYATHINK 40a - BEEBALM 52a - TEAKETTLES 61a - CUETHEMUSIC And then the.. 'reveal': "Modern movement initialism, or a hint to the starts of, 18, 24, 40, 52 and 61across." LGBTQ Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=5/8/2017&h=55d ....
Applause! Love this puzzle with my whole 💖!

Took 20% longer than average to finish.  

It wasn't until I revisited the puzzle that I grasped the theme (I had not read the WordPlay column).  "Mom, Dad, I'm queer."  CAME OUT ON TOP.  Very clever, and an appropriate beginning for the month.

I thought the theme was something to do with going on Queer Eye, “historic” seemed to be overblowing it until it was explained. A very brave step for anyone (unfortunately) and very nicely executed.

I haven’t figured out JAM yet, the clue seems to be plural?

Oh... and didn't see anybody else mention it, but... the 'village in Wales' is...

Llanfairpwllgwyngyllgogerychwyrndrobwllllantysiliogogogoch

Never encountered that before. Quite sure it will never be an answer in a puzzle. 

Oh, and that translates to: 

"St. Mary’s Church in the hollow of the white hazel near a rapid whirlpool and the Church of St. Tysilio near the red cave."

....

Honestly, I didn’t pay attention to the shaded squares while I was doing the puzzle since I was finding this sort of challenging for a Monday (though I managing to finish in around 30% under my average time). So, it wasn’t until I came to the column and read the constructor’s note that I went back to take a look. Understanding the context, I found the puzzle, itself, quite moving. Blessings on Kenneth Cortes, and on anyone who’s had to have an unnecessarily difficult conversation about who they are. I hope the puzzle opens some bridges between those who are gay or trans and the people in their lives.
As a dad, I feel obligated to respond with "Hi Queer, I'm dad!"

I wasn't sure this was actually a serious announcement...
then I thought, "I would hate for this to be the way I found out something as important as this"....
and then I thought (speaking as a parent) it's likely that MOM and DAD already knew, but might not have found a way to initiate a conversation.... 
and then I thought that it's up to these individuals to handle this, and I have no role other than to wish them well and hope that they are led by love.  
Amen.
Wonderful and uplifting puzzle! Happy Pride from a fellow queer (and  crossword addict)!

Pride is a protest. Pride is a party. This article from the Guardian in 2020 is one of the best summaries I've seen of the movement: https://www.theguardian.com/world/2020/jun/25/party-and-protest-lgbtq-radical-history-gay-liberation-stonewall-pride Thank you Kenneth Cortes for a brilliant, fun, and heart-felt kickoff to Pride Month.

There is nothing more meaningful to me to experience someone sharing themselves vulnerably with an audience of strangers. This puzzle absolutely warmed my heart, and I loved what you wrote in the constructors notes.  Thank you. Happy pride everyone!

Lovely. Our youngest prefers Gay to QUEER, I get lost in the convolutions of what is what. We knew, but waited for him to feel comfortable with himself, which he was at age 14.  I think he felt a tad deflated at our complete acceptance of and pride in who he is.  I did offer to throw him out into the snow and never darken our door again, but he said the moment had gone! 

Cute puzzle, straightforward Monday time. Our resident Shiba INU  graciously accepts being in the crossword twice within a couple of weeks.
Well isn't that special, hope it all worked out for you, Kenneth. And thanks for a quality Monday puzzle.
Oh wow, what an amazing puzzle! I'm glad I got to this one early--it was such a wonderful way to start my day!
Beautiful puzzle today.  Happy pride!
this is the best puzzle ever!! what an amazing and joyful way to start pride month :) Congratulations Kenneth!!

I asked myself a lot of questions as I was solving this.

Question 1: What on earth did MOM, DAD, IM and QUEER have in common?

Question 2 (after seeing the revealer):  I've heard of IM OUT and QUEER people come out of the closet, but why are MOM and DAD out?  Beats me.

Oh, wait, I'm a little slow.  It's a statement that says: MOM, DAD, I'M QUEER. Aha!  And the revealer CAME OUT ON TOP is inspired!

But the revealer clue is wrong: It should be what the constructor SAID and not what the constructor DID.  Am I right?

Wondering how Kenneth feels about MOM and DAD?  DAD  BODS are a pejorative in everyone's book.  And MOM JEANS -- far from being a "fashion trend" -- are famous for being UNfashionable.  Hmmm.  Put-downs, both.  Maybe MOM and DAD didn't handle Kenneth's coming out all that gracefully?

At any rate, I enjoyed this puzzle a lot, finding it entertaining, surprising, and much harder than usual for a Monday.
What a wonderfully appropriate puzzle for June! Congratulations, Kenneth!

fabulous puzzle!
congratulations Kenneth!
fabulous pride puzzle Kenneth you got a rare prize for this Rex does the crossword gave you 5 stars that's kinda like getting a crossword Oscar.  Bravo!
Proud of you Kenneth!  Just shared the answers and column and constructor notes with my wife, and we're both beaming for you.

Congratulations, Kenneth, on this fabulous grid and constructor notes that are just perfect for the start of June!! I hope they receive a well-deserved high rating on the CLAPOMETER!👏🏼🏳️‍🌈👏🏼

Happy June and Happy Pride Month, Kenneth & everyone!🌈🫶🏼☮️

Excellent and brave puzzle! In our younger days, my big burly husband and I marched in Atlanta's Pride Parade with the National Organization for Women. Many assumed we were siblings and he appreciated the attention from the Bears. Happy Pride! ❤️🧡💛💚💙💜
Kenny, Kenny, Kenny!!! Your old middle school band director here. Nice job on the puzzle... feeling proud for you, my friend!

Love this puzzle - thank you!

Also heartwarming to see 100 percent positivity on this thread.  (Maybe I missed someone grumbling, but I don’t think so.)

Fantastic puzzle to complete right after hoisting my pride flag for the month!

I feel like - given the theme - 16-across might be a Steven Universe reference? Anyone else get the same feeling?

Congrats on a satisfying-to-solve historic and historical puzzle, Kenneth!

Ok, this one made me laugh out loud.

Well done!

Oh goodness, this makes me so happy!  I'm all choked up now 🥹 Thank you so much for this lovely and touching puzzle, Kenneth, you've made this queer auntie very proud indeed ❤️

Great fill and clues as well, by the way, this was a fun one!
What an awesome way to come out!  Congrats on this epic achievement.  Your folks should be so proud of you.

Despite the Welsh town being in a Sondheim lyric, I have never quite learned how to pronounce it. It's a novelty song he wrote with Mary Rodgers; it's a parody of "The Girl From Ipanema" and was featured in a review called The MAD Show (as in MAD magazine). It's called "The Boy From..." and here's original cast member Linda Lavin singing it on one of the afternoon talk shows, maybe Merv Griffin? https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WFC9uuHcxbA good puzzle! off to read the comments now.

A good puzzle and a wonderful opportunity for virtue signalling in the comments.

Me too.

A delightful puzzle! Smooth clues, clean fill. I've loved seeing the younger generation of constructors inject fresh vocab and slang into the puzzles. And this one is particularly special, one to remember. Hats off to you Kenneth! (On the HATSTAND of course...) 🌈🩷
Thanks Kenneth for a fun Monday puzzle. Your revealer was very good. The Welsh village is not far from my own and it's nice to see a shout out if only tangentially.

I have always been pleased that QUEER, once a pejorative used by the hateful, has been joyfully taken back by the gay community.  If my grade school teachers were correct, the same was done for the word "yankee" during the American Revolution.

So now it is time that we reclaim words like "woke" and "liberal".  Those who despise the left have twisted them into derisive adjectives despite their textbook definitions.  Maybe we need a Woke Pride parade.

I’m going to be pedantic but as a former Latin American historian I need to point out that Panama is technically part of South America, not Central America. The Central American republics (Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua, and Costa Rica) were governed from Mexico City in the colonial period and declared independence together in the 1820s. Panama was a province of Colombia, initially governed from Lima, then from Bogota. It remained part of Colombia until the US encouraged it to declare independence in 1903 in order to build the canal.

I don’t normally do the normal crossword as I get frustrated with myself and don’t need another reason to feel bad about myself 😅 in saying that, I was moved to have a go today given the teaser. As someone in the queer community, this warmed my heart. I was lucky enough to have two very supportive parents when I came out. My 80yo Mum still wears her “Free Mum Hugs” rainbow pin and is fiercely protective of the community. I hope the crossword constructor is happy, as we all wish to be.
Nice to have a little challenge in a Monday puzzle.
This puzzle makes this old gay guy smile. My husband and I are on our way to Provincetown to celebrate our 39th year together. Happy Pride indeed!🏳️‍🌈
Should have made my parents do this puzzle 15 years ago 😂😂
Yeah, best Monday puzzle ever, and a big Mom Hug to Kenneth!
ALAN Menken is also a nice little tie to the theme.
I wasn't expecting to cry while solving a crossword today (or ever, really) and yet here we are
Ahem-- even the "modern" gay rights movement began in 1969 at Stonewall, not the 70s, but when I participated in the first planned demo in 1964, the movement was well under  way!

Off topic, but, why does the Times not include the Wordplay for older puzzles? I love to do them, but they should be complete with the same info the current puzzles have. 

Maybe set up a section for people to do the "vintage puzzles?"

The New York Times Diverse Crossword Constructor Fellowship provides mentorship and support for constructors whose backgrounds and perspectives could be better reflected in our puzzles. We want our puzzles to reflect the experiences of as many people as possible, which means publishing work that displays a wide range of cultural reference points, language usage and communities. The fellowship is for constructors who have not yet been published by The New York Times. Applications for this year's fellowship open June 15. The class will begin in September. More information is available at the link below. https://www.nytimes.com/2025/03/17/crosswords/do-you-want-to-learn-how-to-make-a-crossword.html
Bravo, Kenneth! You made me get teary, too, and that’s a first for a crossword session. Please add a Mom Hug from me to the string!
From one queer cruciverbalist to another, happy pride. 🌈

I almost didn’t read the comments for fear of being sucked into a wormhole of hate and ignorance. To say I was most pleasantly surprised would be an understatement! The love and support shown here is truly inspiring.

Kenneth, congrats on not only knowing yourself, but loving yourself and being proud of who you are! I pray everyone, no matter their orientation, may be as fortunate!
Above average solve time for a Monday, because once I got the theme I had to do the rest of the puzzle with tears in my eyes. I love this so much. Congratulations, Kenneth, and happy Pride, everyone!
Love love this crossword. Congrats, Kenneth!
First crossword to ever make me tear up. 🥹Congratulations on a wonderful puzzle, and on this momentous moment in your life, Kenneth 🌈
Simply fab.
As a daily NYT puzzle solver, this one... chef's kiss. <3
Bravo! Love and support 🌈❤️
Fabulous puzzle that got me misty-eyed. Congrats Kenneth and Happy Pride Month!🌈

I'm pretty certain that my teenage daughter is realizing her attraction to females. The comments in this forum warm my heart to know that her choice will not be met with the same vitriol that many of the older commenters had to face.  Thank you all. Cheers.
Lind Lavin has the blueth: https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=WFC9uuHcxbA&list=RDWFC9uuHcxbA&start_radio=1&pp=ygUVc29uZGhlaW0gdGhlIGJveSBmcm9toAcB (stay around for the last line.)

Great puzzle.
And even today's Google doodle is marking Pride Month.

I admire and support this community's striving to foster kindness and respect for ALL. Vive la différence! We also celebrate an especial oneness--an identity, if you will: our love for the greatest Crossword in the land. Let us therefore arrest this nascent impulse to draw contentious political elements into our beloved puzzle. Which elements might so qualify could be debated ad infinitum, but, like it or lump it, the NYT Crossword community ranges across the entire political spectrum, and ALL should here enjoy a safe haven from the baleful divisions that so rend our extracruciverbal lives. Ad astra!
I love this so much.
Fun puzzle!

What!?!? That's awesome 🥹🏳️‍🌈🏳️‍⚧️💖🌈

Happy June!
Hahahaha- very clever and fun Monday!
How fun! Thank you!

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle
a/ everything wants everything with this pearl you burn the surface of the universe with this praise you accede
d/ cut me out of the show place me in a country in the wood walk with me

Happy Pride Month from a queer Black trans resident of San Francisco, city of the Compton's Cafeteria Riot in 1966, predating the Stonewall Rebellion by three years. The location is now home to the world's first Transgender District. https://www.theguardian.com/lifeandstyle/2019/jun/21/stonewall-san-francisco-riot-tenderloin-neighborhood-trans-women https://transgenderdistrictsf.com/

please share this pertinent data. solved the Times puzzles since 1986 https://linktr.ee/glassjonespiano readers must KNOW https://www.humandignitytrust.org/lgbt-the-law/map-of-criminalisation/ 65 NATIONS jurisdictions criminalise private, consensual, same-sex sexual activity. The majority of these jurisdictions explicitly criminalise sex between men via ‘sodomy’, ‘buggery’ and ‘unnatural offences’ laws. Almost half of them are Commonwealth jurisdictions. 41 NATIONS countries criminalise private, consensual sexual activity between women using laws against ‘lesbianism’, ‘sexual relations with a person of the same sex’ and ‘gross indecency’. Even in jurisdictions that do not explicitly criminalise women, lesbians and bisexual women have been subjected to arrest or threat of arrest. 12 NATIONS countries have jurisdictions in which the death penalty is imposed or at least a possibility for private, consensual same-sex sexual activity. At least 6 of these implement the death penalty – Iran, Northern Nigeria, Saudi Arabia, Somalia and Yemen – and the death penalty is a legal possibility in Afghanistan, Brunei, Mauritania, Pakistan, Qatar, UAE and Uganda. 13 NATIONS countries criminalise the gender identity and/or expression of transgender people, using so-called ‘cross-dressing’, ‘impersonation’ and ‘disguise’ laws. In many more countries transgender people are targeted by a range of laws that criminalise same-sex activity and vagrancy, hooliganism and public order offences.

commentBody: @lucky13 There's a great set of three questions from Jewish tradition. The Jewish sage Hillel the Elder recommended asking: If I am not for me, who will be for me? If I am only for me, what am I? If not now, when? The late Canadian diplomat/politician Bob Rae used them as a template for a deep book called "The Three Questions: Prosperity and the Public Good." Read more here: https://archive.org/details/threequestionspr0000raeb_n6l2/mode/1up If I had to be a politician (which God forbid!), I would have wanted to be Bob Rae.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Francis

I have expressed myself poorly.

Not about "problem," more about "chew."

Take all the clue/fills from yesterday and put them in column S.  Give each one a level-of-difficulty score.  This is dS  Now do the same for today.  Call them column M and dM.  Now sum the scores of dS and dM and divide each by the column's respective number of clues.

I felt mean dM > mean dS.

Still just me?  Okay.

In an unrelated esthetic matter, I have always strongly disagreed with thinkers from Marx to Morrison that all art is political, believing that the collision/collusion of the two more often than not either distracts from the actual high quality of the piece (rare) or artificially exalts its mediocrity (far more common).

Here is an example of the former, imo.

This was an excellent Monday.

And I fear the cleverness of its construction might be diluted by the courage of its creator.

Talk about what makes you you...that's really what makes me me.

I have spent a lot of my professional life/lives explaining things — either on my feet or in writing. But, except here where nobody knows me, I wouldn't dream of speaking without being asked. I suppose people will inevitably grow tired of me or I'll start to lose the thread of my topic too many times and then the invitations and the jobs will dry up. commentBody: For those who don’t know the school was founded in the late 1800’s and the name is a poor transliteration of the Hebrew word for “oak” - אלון or Ălon. Not sure how the “A” became an “E.”
@Petrol That university existed before MUSKrat.

userLocation: Doubt exists only in thought–not action.
commentBody:
@Matt
Seems to be based on a misunderstanding of the plural appositive form: /eilonei/.  (Sorry, I can't do the Hebrew letters.) The expression /eilonei mamre/ = the oaks of Mamre appears in Genesis 14:13. Alon (sing. nom.) becomes eilonim (pl. nom.), which becomes eilonei (pl. appos.) in biblical Hebrew. Somewhere along the centuries and the transition from Hebrew to Greek to Latin, the vocalization of eilon- as the plural form was read back into the singular. Et voilà!

@Miranda Yes, this went straight to my heart too! It’s unusual to have an emotional response to a crossword, so kudos to Kenneth and best wishes to him, his family and all his community! 🏳️‍🌈🏳️‍⚧️
@Miranda there's nothing less meaningful if you ask me. Internet interaction is impersonal and honestly pretty selfish just trying to get attention and clicks and praise.

@Todd

It certainly can be. It can also be a lifesaver, especially when dealing with issues that are marginalized or stigmatized. 

One can find a community and support online that doesn't exist in their physical community. One can learn that they're not alone in their struggles and have others to face them with. 

This has been my experience with certain issues, and I know many others as well.

Like so many things, I think the internet is what you make of it. After all, in-person interactions can be just as impersonal and selfish... Or fake.  They can be just as attention seeking.

@Todd

I thought that before I discovered this forum.

Now I'm convinced that internet interactions can be a real as any other kind. 

The praise and clicks are a part of it, to be sure, but it's no different than craving a feeling of belonging in any other group.  When one is in person, one looks for facial signs of understanding and possibly amusement.  That really no different.

@Helen Wright

You made me laugh :D

@Helen Wright
Hope the Alpacas aren't jealous.

@Vaer
They’re still sulking with me as I subjected them to TB testing and shearing.

@Helen Wright

It must be a strange sensation to come out, and realize that everyone around you already knew and had accepted that about you.

The constructor's note mentions that like gender, coming out is also not necessarily binary. This has been true for me, too. 

I'm a cis man who is twenty-some years into a happy marriage with a cis woman, but I am and have always been queer. It only comes up when I make a point of mentioning it (as I am doing right now). So I'm grateful to Mr. Cortes not only for this wonderful puzzle, but also for giving me an occasion to openly be myself.
@Nancy I would say the constructor went on to live a happy and fulfilled life after that nerve wracking time - he came out on top. So that clue is correct.

@Nancy Actually, dad bods have taken on a positive meaning recently: https://toddkashdan.substack.com/p/dad-bod-as-a-sign-of-status-and-personality As did the somewhat related "beekeeping age": https://www.urbandictionary.com/define.php?term=Beekeeping+Age Mom jeans are ugly but definitely fashionable...

@Andrzej You know, I've thought about this before. And I don't intend this as a critique of the puzzle, but I feel like these terms indicate a common American double-standard: Mom jeans, which spotlight middle-aged (or not young) women's bodies, are an object of derision ("Karen"). Dad bods, which spotlight middle-aged men's bodies, are, as you point out, an asset. (Seth Rogan, Paul Rudd, etc.). I don't put too much weight on this arbitrary comparison, but the terms do seem to provide an apt articulation of our sexism.

@Bob T.
Thanks for the link!  An inspiration to learn this, along with a few of the incredible Scripps Spelling Bee words from last week!

@Bob T. thanks so much for that blast of 70s nostalgia! The set, the font and Linda Lavin's ensemble! It was the Mike Douglas Show, btw. My Mom never watched the soaps, but she was a big fan of both Merv and Mike.

@Steve L

Exactly right.

The whole idea of "woke" is that someone believed something, discovered new facts, and "woke" up to the fact that their original position was wrong.

What is the world is wrong with that?  I call that becoming an adult, which may be why so many men-children and women-children can't accept it.

@Andy Most Latin American historians would probably call Panama part of America, as it is the custom to consider the entire New World landmass one continent. But there are various ways of dividing the world into continents, and here in the US, we do generally divide the Americas by North and South. To make matters more confusing, we distinguish Central America as well, but consider it part of North America, not a separate continent. Which brings us to Panama. Generally, Panama is considered part of Central America by geographers in the US. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_America https://www.britannica.com/place/Central-America https://kids.nationalgeographic.com/geography/countries/article/panama Indeed, historically there were federations containing "Central America" in their names, consisting of the other five Spanish-speaking Central American countries, or of three of them, none of which were Panama, because it was at the time part of Colombia. But that is a historical context only, and it doesn't mean that Panama is part of South America. Ownership by a South American country doesn't make it part of South America. Would you argue that Hawaii is in North America?

@Andy

I’m not an historian, but my suspicion is there’s a difference between the political distinction you are making and a geographical one. My cursory research suggests there’s a tectonic plate divide between Panama and Columbia and that Panama lies on the North American side, so I suspect a geologist would disagree with you.

@Catherine

You asked this the other day, and I answered you.

Wordplay has existed since 2008.  The articles are still available.  You can Google "wordplay NYT" (plus the date).

If this doesn't work for you, you can go to the complete archive at xwordinfo.com and find the puzzle you're interested in.  Click it open and there will be a link to the corresponding Wordplay above the grid.  This is a pay site, but you can go this far without being asked to join and pay.
